title: Apex AI
tagline: Vision-only AI race engineer — no sensors, just video
description: Upload any onboard karting or motorcycle lap video and Apex AI watches it like a race engineer. It finds every driving mistake with timestamps, highlights your best moments, and generates a professional coaching report — in seconds.
longDescription: Built at the Voxel51 Hackathon (April 2026) with team NUPitlane. The pipeline runs Twelve Labs Pegasus for whole-video semantic analysis, then Groq LLaMA 3.3 70B to synthesize structured coaching output. Available as both a FiftyOne plugin and a standalone React web app. Won the hackathon.
highlights: Watches the entire lap with Twelve Labs Pegasus — understands racing context, not just frames
Finds braking errors, missed apexes, wide exits, and throttle issues with exact timestamps
Generates performance scores (1–10) for Racing Line, Braking, and Throttle
Lap time loss estimates in tenths of seconds per mistake
Works for karting AND motorcycles — biking adds lean angle & body position analysis
50 free analyses/day on Twelve Labs free tier
challenges: Twelve Labs returns rich but unstructured observations. Getting Groq LLaMA to reliably extract valid JSON performance scores from those observations — without hallucinating metrics — required careful prompt engineering and a validation layer that retries on parse failure.
learnings: Multimodal video AI is a fundamentally different abstraction than frame-by-frame CV. Pegasus understands racing strategy and driver intent, not just pixel motion. That semantic understanding is what makes the coaching useful rather than just technically correct.

title: Edge AI Inference Engine
tagline: On-device ML inference for resource-constrained hardware
description: Lightweight inference engine that runs quantized neural networks on microcontrollers and embedded Linux boards — no cloud, no connectivity required. Targets real-time object detection and keyword spotting at under 100mW.
longDescription: The engine implements INT8 quantization, layer fusion, and memory-efficient operator scheduling to fit MobileNetV3 and Tiny-YOLO variants inside 512KB of RAM. Benchmarked across Raspberry Pi Zero 2W, STM32H7, and ESP32-S3. Achieved 28 FPS object detection on Pi Zero 2W — 3× faster than TFLite Micro baseline.
highlights: INT8 quantization + layer fusion — 3× faster than TFLite Micro baseline
28 FPS object detection on Raspberry Pi Zero 2W (512MB RAM)
Runs on STM32H7 and ESP32-S3 with <100mW power draw
Custom memory allocator reduces peak RAM by 40% vs. naive approach
challenges: Memory layout on microcontrollers is extremely constrained — no heap fragmentation allowed. I built a static arena allocator that packs layer activations into a single contiguous buffer, reusing memory across layers where data flow permits.
learnings: Low-level optimization is mostly about understanding your target's memory hierarchy. Cache misses dominate latency on embedded Cortex-A cores, not arithmetic. Rearranging tensor layout gave a bigger speedup than any algorithmic change.

title: 6-DOF Robot Arm
description: Designed, built, and programmed a 6-degree-of-freedom robot arm capable of picking and placing objects using a depth camera and custom grasp planning algorithm.
longDescription: The arm was designed in Fusion 360 and 3D-printed in PLA. Each joint uses a Dynamixel servo with position feedback. The vision pipeline runs YOLOv8 for object detection and uses a point cloud from an Intel RealSense D435 to estimate 3D pose. ROS2 handles communication between the perception node, IK solver, and hardware controller.
highlights: Custom 6-DOF inverse kinematics solver with singularity avoidance
YOLOv8 + RealSense D435 for 6DOF object pose estimation
Sub-centimeter pick accuracy on 20+ object classes
Built and designed in-house — no commercial robot kit used
challenges: Calibrating the camera-to-robot transform (hand-eye calibration) took far longer than expected. Small errors compound across the kinematic chain — a 2mm calibration error becomes 8mm at the end-effector after 6 joints.
learnings: Mechanical tolerances matter enormously in robotics. I learned to design for printability and assembly, not just function — slots for wire routing, accessible screw heads, and snap-fit joints make a real difference.

title: Cisco Network Automation
tagline: Config management & deployment pipeline for enterprise networks
description: Internal tooling built at Cisco to automate network device configuration management. Reduced manual deployment steps by 60% and eliminated a class of configuration drift bugs affecting 1,000+ devices.
longDescription: The system pulls desired-state configs from a Git repo, diffs them against live device states via NETCONF, and generates a deployment plan. A Kafka-based event bus streams telemetry from 10,000+ daily network events to a dashboard that surfaces anomalies before they become incidents.
highlights: Automated config deployment across 1,000+ network devices
NETCONF-based desired-state diffing eliminates configuration drift
Kafka pipeline processing 10,000+ telemetry events per day
Reduced manual deployment steps by 60% — verified by team metrics
challenges: Network devices don't have uniform APIs — some supported NETCONF, others only SSH with screen-scraping. Building a unified abstraction layer that worked across 4 different device generations was architecturally the most interesting part.
learnings: Enterprise infrastructure engineering is 80% backwards compatibility. I learned to design abstractions that accommodate legacy constraints without leaking them into the core logic.

title: Star Tracker Mount
description: Precision motorized telescope mount that compensates for Earth's rotation, enabling long-exposure astrophotography without star trails. Controlled via custom firmware on an ESP32.
longDescription: The mount uses two stepper motors on RA and Dec axes, driven by a TMC2209 driver for near-silent operation. The ESP32 firmware reads a GPS module for precise sidereal rate calculation and supports Bluetooth control. At 300mm focal length, it holds stars as points for 60+ second exposures.
highlights: GPS-synchronized sidereal tracking rate accurate to <1 arcsec/min
Silent TMC2209 stepper drivers — no vibration during exposures
60+ second exposures at 300mm focal length without star trails
Bluetooth control via custom iOS shortcut app
challenges: Mechanical backlash in the worm gear caused periodic error — a slow oscillation visible at high magnification. Solved it with a PEC (Periodic Error Correction) routine that measures and pre-compensates the gear's error signature.
learnings: Embedded systems demand extreme resource discipline. Running GPS parsing, stepper control, and BLE simultaneously on an ESP32 required careful task prioritization with FreeRTOS to avoid jitter in the tracking loop.

title: Robot Swarm Coordination
tagline: Multi-agent path planning for autonomous robot swarms
description: Research project implementing decentralized coordination algorithms for a swarm of 8 ground robots. Each robot has only local sensing but the group achieves global coverage and collision-free navigation.
longDescription: Implemented three coordination algorithms — potential fields, flocking (Reynolds rules), and a novel hybrid approach — and benchmarked them on coverage, collision rate, and communication overhead. The hybrid method outperformed prior approaches by 23% on the coverage metric in obstacle-dense environments.
highlights: 8-robot hardware testbed with custom ROS2 communication stack
Novel hybrid coordination algorithm outperforms baselines by 23%
Decentralized — each robot acts only on local sensor data
Results submitted to ICRA 2025 workshop
challenges: Real-world robot behavior diverges from simulation in subtle ways — wheel slip, sensor noise, and latency. Tuning algorithm parameters that work in both Gazebo and on the physical testbed required a systematic sim-to-real transfer methodology.
learnings: Multi-agent systems are hard to debug because emergent failures aren't attributable to a single agent. I built per-agent logging and a post-hoc visualizer to reconstruct what each robot perceived at each timestep.
